anchor: ChatGPT Pro and Business unlimited GPT-5 access in ChatGPT is guardrail-based rather than truly uncapped.
note: Official plan docs still frame GPT-5 access in ChatGPT as unlimited or effectively unlimited subject to abuse guardrails. Methods: openai-pro-plan:browser-capture, openai-business-limits:browser-capture.

anchor: GPT-5.4 is the current frontier model for professional work with a 1,050,000 token context window.
note: The latest GPT-5.4 model page no longer exposes the earlier frontier or 1.05M context wording clearly enough to keep the old anchor unchanged. Methods: openai-gpt-5.4-model:direct-fetch.

anchor: GPT-5.4 is available everywhere you use Codex.
note: Codex-facing docs still emphasize GPT-5-Codex or GPT-5.3-Codex rather than proving GPT-5.4 as the universal Codex default. Methods: openai-codex-upgrades:browser-capture, openai-gpt-5.3-codex:browser-capture, openai-gpt-5.4-model:direct-fetch.

anchor: Codex-native frontier work currently rides on GPT-5.3-Codex or GPT-5-Codex rather than a public GPT-5.4 Codex release.
note: Current Codex-native docs still center GPT-5.3-Codex or GPT-5-Codex on the Codex surface. Methods: openai-gpt-5.3-codex:browser-capture, openai-gpt-5.4-model:direct-fetch.

anchor: Codex supports ChatGPT sign-in and is included with ChatGPT plans.
note: Current Codex docs still support ChatGPT sign-in and plan-based access. Methods: openai-codex-plan:browser-capture, openai-codex-upgrades:browser-capture.

anchor: Current Codex Pro local usage windows are higher than the prompt's 223-1120 estimate.
note: The current Codex plan article could not be parsed cleanly enough to restate the present local-window numbers automatically. Methods: openai-codex-plan:browser-capture.

anchor: OpenClaw officially supports Codex OAuth / ChatGPT sign-in.
note: OpenClaw provider docs still explicitly document OpenAI Codex OAuth support. Methods: openclaw-model-providers:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw recommends Node runtime for stable gateways and does not recommend Bun for gateway installs.
note: The current CLI docs could not be parsed cleanly enough to restate the Node-versus-Bun guidance automatically. Methods: openclaw-cli:direct-fetch.

anchor: The strongest always-on OpenClaw pattern is one primary gateway with remote nodes and clients.
note: The single-gateway guidance needs a human check because the latest pages did not expose the wording clearly enough. Methods: openclaw-multiple-gateways:direct-fetch, openclaw-faq:direct-fetch.

anchor: Cron and heartbeat serve different purposes and should be combined.
note: The latest automation docs still distinguish cron from heartbeat and recommend using both together. Methods: openclaw-cron-vs-heartbeat:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw memory is Markdown-first, and Codex OAuth does not satisfy embeddings for memory search.
note: The latest memory docs still show Markdown-first memory and separate embedding requirements. Methods: openclaw-memory:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw supports QMD and local memory-search backends.
note: The memory docs still advertise local-first memory search options such as QMD or local embeddings. Methods: openclaw-memory:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw skills, plugins, and skill folders are supply-chain risk and must be treated as trusted or untrusted code accordingly.
note: OpenClaw security guidance still treats skills and plugins as trusted-code or supply-chain risk surfaces. Methods: openclaw-security:direct-fetch, openclaw-secrets:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw current docs expose strict config validation, additive SecretRefs, and a first-class PDF tool.
note: Current OpenClaw docs still expose config validation or doctor-style checks, broader secret coverage, and PDF tooling. Methods: openclaw-configuration:direct-fetch, openclaw-secrets:direct-fetch, openclaw-pdf:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw can use a managed browser or an attached Chrome relay, and headless mode is more likely to trigger anti-bot checks.
note: The browser docs still cover managed browsing, attached Chrome, and headless tradeoffs. Methods: openclaw-browser:direct-fetch, openclaw-faq:direct-fetch, openclaw-browser-login:direct-fetch.

anchor: Automated self-update is possible but not the primary recommended operating pattern.
note: The latest FAQ needs a human confirmation of the self-update guidance. Methods: openclaw-faq:direct-fetch.

anchor: Wise personal tokens and OAuth 2.0 support different automation realities, and EU/UK PSD2 constrains personal-token actions.
note: Current Wise docs still separate personal-token and OAuth realities and preserve PSD2 constraints. Methods: wise-security-access:direct-fetch, wise-personal-tokens:direct-fetch, wise-getting-started:direct-fetch.

anchor: GPT-4.5-preview assumptions are stale and deprecated.
note: The latest release notes need a human review before restating the GPT-4.5 deprecation claim. Methods: openai-model-release-notes:browser-capture, openai-gpt-5.4-model:direct-fetch.
